steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Wade's argument has two claims: (1) the three pieces come from a minstrel's repertoire book, and (2) that minstrel worked in the area around Heege's home. Supporting evidence must address both claims, confirming the pieces are linked to live performance for a local audience near Heege's home.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Heege's collection contains the earliest examples of the three pieces in question, but each of those pieces occurs in other manuscript collections compiled after Heege's.’: This describes manuscript transmission history, not live performance or local geography. It neither confirms a minstrel origin nor a local-area connection. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Traveling minstrels are thought to have performed mainly for elite audiences, and other parts of Heege's collection reflect his interest in works with popular appeal.’: This actually creates tension with Wade's argument by implying the pieces may have popular (non-elite) appeal that doesn't fit the minstrel profile described, and it doesn't connect the pieces to Heege's local area. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Features like repetition also occur in other texts from the period that were widely read but are not known to have been performed for live audiences.’: This directly weakens Wade's argument by suggesting the live-performance features he cites could belong to written texts rather than performance repertoire. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘The three pieces in question contain references that presume that the audience is familiar with Radford and other small villages near where Heege lived.’: Local place-name references only make sense if the audience knows those villages — exactly what a minstrel performing for local residents near Heege's home would include. This supports both the minstrel-performance claim and the local-area claim. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
